text:
# Page 1^page=1
Line item approver implementation User Flow regardless of implementation: Current questions to get answered that could change design Do requisitioners need to verify removal before requisition submission? yes - are they allowed to modify the requisition after an item is removed? (ie. add the correct item if it was incorrect) - if modified, do we send back to the approver? is it the same exact process? no - How would a requisitioner solve the issue of an incorrectly added item if it was removed by the approver and edits are not allowed? should editability be conditional based on reason code? 1. Approver clicks "Remove" on a line item 2. Confirmation modal appears 3. Approver selects reason code from dropdown and adds optional comments 4. On confirmation, item is marked as removed in the database and quantity set to 0. 5. UI updates to show item as removed (shaded yellow with an undo button displayed) 6. Approver can UNDO removal if needed, and the yellow tint and undo button are removed 7. On save, email notification is sent to requisitioner

# Page 2^page=2
Do requisitioners see the removed item highlighted in yellow , or do they not see the item? Yes - Do they see the reason code and comments on the page as well? No - How does the requisitioner know what items are removed and why? do they need to refer back to the email? Is submission to SMAR T after approval the only downstream service that needs requisition data? Yes - No changes to flow , this assumption was made No - How do we ensure that the removed items are not included in the requisition ingestion? Use RequisitionItemStatusHistories Table with Dual-Safety Approach How It Works When an approver removes a line item, we create a new status history record with a "Removed by Approver" status AND set the quantity to zero. This provides two safety mechanisms to prevent removed items from being submitted to SMAR T Legacy: This dual-safety approach ensures removed items don't reach SMAR T Legacy even if one mechanism fails. Database Changes RequisitionServices Changes 1. Zero quantity items are already filtered out by existing code 2. Items with "Removed by Approver" status will be explicitly filtered 1. Add new status type to dbo.RequisitionItemStatusT ypes: "Removed by Approver" status No table changes necessary

# Page 3^page=3
1. Add new entry in RequisitionItemStatusT ype: RemovedByApprover 2. Use existing RequisitionItemStatusHistories table to track removal: Set item status to "Removed by Approver" Store reason code and comments in the Comments field "Reason: [ReasonCode] - [Comments]" 3. Store original quantity in the status history record for potential undo 4. Set item quantity to zero when removed (leveraging existing zero- quantity filtering) 5. Add this or a similar filter for "Removed by Approver" status in SubmitRequisitionT oSmart method: // Add this method to filter out removed items privateIEnumerable<RequisitionItem> RemoveRemovedByApproverItems(IEnumerable<RequisitionItem> items) { if(items ==null) returnnull; return items.Where(item => item.RequisitionItemStatusTypeId != (int)RequisitionItemStatusTypeEnum.RemovedByApprover); } publicRequisitionDTOSubmitRequisitionToSmart(string userName,string cartId,RequisitionDTO requisitionDTO) { // Convert DTO to domain model var requisition = requisitionMapper.Map<RequisitionDTO, Requisition>(requisitionDTO);

# Page 4^page=4
ProcurementServices Changes try { // First filter out items removed by approvers requisition.RequisitionItems = this.RemoveRemovedByApproverItems(requisition.RequisitionItems ); // Then remove zero quantity items (existing functionality) requisition.RequisitionItems = this.RemoveZeroQuantityLineItems(requisition.RequisitionItems) ; // Continue with SMART Legacy submission... } // ... } 1. New Endpoint RemoveRequisitionItem(requisitionId, requisitionItemId, reasonCode, comments) Validates approver permissions Sets item quantity to zero Sets item status to "Removed by Approver" Stores original quantity for potential undo Calls RequisitionServices to implement removal Returns success/failure response UndoRemoveRequisitionItem(requisitionId, requisitionItemId) Restores original quantity from status history

# Page 5^page=5
Web Changes Restores previous status Adds new status history record 2. Email Notification Implementation: EmailTypeEnum.cs - create new email type EmailConstants.cs - Add new constants: EmailHeadline.LineItemsRemoved EmailBodyMessage.LineItemsRemoved EmailSubject.LineItemsRemoved BodyTemplatePath.LineItemsRemoved EmailRequest.cs - Add fields: ApproverName, RemovedItemsList Create new HTML template with tokens for removed items list EmailService.cs - Add token replacement RequisitionService.cs - Create method that builds EmailRequest and calls emailService.SendEmail() 1. Approval Details Controller Add removeRequisitionItem function Display confirmation modal Update UI after successful removal 2. ApprovalReqReview Controller Add same removeRequisitionItem function for approval review modal 3. RequisitionItemList.html Add "Remove" button for approvers Show removed items with visual distinction (shaded/yellowed out) Display undo button when an item is removed Add condition to check if item is removed in cost calculation 4. Confirmation Modal

# Page 6^page=6
General Business Logic Current flow Create new removal confirmation modal with reason code dropdown and comments field Include "cancel" functionality When an approver removes an item: Create a new status history record with "Removed by Approver" status Set item quantity to zero Store original quantity in status history for undo Store removal reason comments field: "Reason: [ReasonCode] - [Comments]" The UI displays these items as removed (shaded yellow) and the undo button appears UNDO functionality: Restores original quantity from status history Adds a new status history record with previous status Updates UI to remove yellow tint and undo button Requisition total excludes items with "Removed by Approver" status SMART Legacy submission: First filters out items with "Removed by Approver" status Then applies existing zero-quantity filter as secondary safety

# Page 7^page=7
Proposed flow
